rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions (the top 8 and bottom 13 of the original ranking); ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank and the published set is a bimodal artifact, not a representative distribution. Ohio does not currently appear in the published index at all and must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt — see rank_note). Calibrated to the methodology's dimension anchors, then sanity-checked against named published peers (Indiana/Missouri 25.0 with all dimensions flat 2.0; Connecticut 83.0 mostly 4.0; Louisiana 21.9, Alabama/Mississippi 12.5) and against the other five states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Ohio 38.1, Michigan 51.9 (superseded, corrected 53.8), Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9. Ohio scores above the Indiana/Missouri cluster because it operates real, dated, published detection and outcome machinery those files do not evidence (Pregnancy-Associated Mortality Review, annual Infant Mortality Report, OhioRISE utilization outcomes); it scores far below Connecticut because it lacks independent verification of its own performance and removed an oversight body (CIIC) during the assessment window. Composite verified with computeCompositeFromDimensions in site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs: dimension mean 2.525, baseComposite 38.125, stdDev 0.2222 (consistencyMult 1.0), 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Function returns {"composite":38.1,"band":"Developing"} — matches the target exactly.
attribution_note: Scores Ohio's own state conduct. HB 96 (June 2025) enacted a state-legislated trigger automatically ending Medicaid expansion coverage for roughly 770,000 Ohioans if the federal matching rate falls below 90%; the trigger mechanism itself, and the absence of a published transition plan for it, is Ohio's own legislative design choice and is scored (EQU/BND), separate from the federal matching-rate policy that could someday activate it. Ohio's continued retention of Medicaid expansion throughout the assessment period is likewise a state choice and is credited (EQU1). SB 1 (closing university DEI offices, banning faculty strikes) and HB 68 (banning gender-affirming care for minors, enacted over the Governor's veto) are entirely state-originated and fully scored. The 2022 redistricting defiance — the state running elections on legislative maps its own Ohio Supreme Court had five times ruled unconstitutional — is state conduct scored at ACC/INT; no federal actor was involved. Governor DeWine's September 2024 defense of Springfield's Haitian residents against his own party's national ticket is scored as state-executive conduct (I1), distinct from the federal-level political rhetoric it responded to.
key_evidence: Ohio saves more lives than it used to and answers for itself less: predicted overdose deaths fell from 4,847 (Dec 2023) to 3,136 (Dec 2024), a decline of more than 35%, while in the same period the state abolished its independent prison oversight body (CIIC, defunded and abolished June 2025) and walked away from the costed Fair School Funding Plan.

Ohio's own reviews publish damning numbers and the state does not fund the fix: the Pregnancy-Associated Mortality Review found 66% of pregnancy-related deaths preventable and Black infants die at 2.5 times the rate of white infants, while the Fair School Funding Plan's costed $3.04 billion gap was funded at only $281.9 million, leaving $2.75 billion unfunded and cutting the highest-poverty districts by $34.6 million.

Between January and May 2022 the Ohio Supreme Court struck down five successive sets of legislative maps as unconstitutional partisan gerrymanders; the Redistricting Commission never produced a lawful map and the November 2022 midterms proceeded anyway. Ohio has never acknowledged or repaired this, and it is the single heaviest drag on Accountability and Integrity.

Ohio's clearest documented compassion is concentrated in one term-limited officeholder: Governor DeWine defended Springfield's Haitian residents against his own party's presidential ticket, vetoed the ban on gender-affirming care for minors (overridden by the Legislature), quietly commuted a death sentence, and on 2026-06-16 called for abolishing the death penalty. DeWine leaves office January 2027 and little of this is embedded in policy.

After the $60 million HB 6 bribery scheme that sent former House Speaker Larry Householder to prison for 20 years, Ohio regulators ordered FirstEnergy utilities to pay $250.7 million on 2025-11-19, including $186.6 million refunded to customers — Ohio's clearest act of concrete repair in the period, arriving five years after the scheme was exposed.
